title: Soy jar candle — sandalwood and cedarwood
subtitle: A warm, woody candle with a long, slow fragrance throw
excerpt: A soy-wax container candle scented with sandalwood and cedarwood essential oils. Both are heavy, slow-releasing base notes that give an even, sustained fragrance rather than a sharp burst. The combination is warm without being sweet.
sourceNotes: Flash points: sandalwood essential oil typically above 90°C; cedarwood essential oil above 60°C. Both well above the soy container pour temperature. Soy container wax fragrance load 6 to 8%: National Candle Association guidelines. Woody essential oil retention in soy wax: general candlemaking technical documentation.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 jar candle (~200 ml, approximately 30 hours burn time)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale wax and fragrance proportionally per jar.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Allow at least 24 hours before lighting; two weeks of cure gives the best cold throw for heavy woody oils.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Store away from sunlight and heat. Woody essential oils are slow-releasing and hold their fragrance in soy wax longer than citrus or herbal oils, often 2 to 3 years before any noticeable fade.

glossaryTerms.definition: The temperature at which a fragrance or essential oil can ignite near an open flame. Sandalwood and cedarwood have high flash points, well above any standard pour temperature.
The first burn of a jar candle, long enough to melt the wax surface to the jar edge and prevent tunnelling on subsequent burns.
The rest period after pouring during which the fragrance binds to the wax matrix. Heavy base-note oils benefit especially from a long cure.

content.text: Sandalwood and cedarwood are both heavy, high-boiling-point essential oils, which makes them good choices for soy wax candles. Lighter, more volatile oils can burn off quickly in a hot wax pool; woody base notes release slowly and evenly, giving a sustained fragrance through the full burn. The scent combination is warm and understated: sandalwood contributes a creamy, smooth quality; cedarwood adds a drier, more resinous edge. Both have
values comfortably above the soy container pour temperature, which simplifies the process. The fragrance load is 7% of wax weight. Two weeks of
is especially worthwhile for heavy base-note oils in soy wax; they need time to bind fully and the cold throw is noticeably stronger after curing.

items.prepNote: 4.5% of wax weight; smooth, creamy base note
2.5% of wax weight; dry, resinous base note
Sized to jar diameter; CD-12 for a 200 ml jar

content.text: Warm the jar briefly to prevent frosting. Secure the wick tab to the jar base and thread the wick through the centring bar resting across the jar opening.

Melt the soy wax in the pouring pitcher over low heat. Do not leave unattended; keep a fire blanket within reach. Remove from heat when fully melted. Let the wax cool to 60 to 65°C on the probe thermometer. Add both essential oils and stir to combine. Pour into the prepared jar, leaving a 1 cm gap at the top. Do not disturb for at least 4 hours. Top up any sink hole around the wick with a small second pour at the same temperature once the first pour has set.
Trim the wick to 5 mm. Burn until the
is complete, with the melt pool reaching the jar edge all the way around. Sandalwood and cedarwood release slowly; the hot throw builds after the wax pool is fully established.
Store away from sunlight and heat. Woody oils are among the most stable in soy wax; the fragrance holds well for 2 to 3 years. Keep the lid on when not in use to protect the cold throw.
